risk_rationale: Public financial-services company with strong disclosure coverage.
Brokerage, crypto, futures, event contracts, private markets, advisory, and digital banking create a broad regulated perimeter.
Bitstamp integration adds global crypto exchange and licensing exposure.
business_model: Retail brokerage and investing platform.
Crypto trading and crypto asset platform activity.
Advisory, digital banking, private markets access, futures and event-contract products.
Bitstamp acquisition expands crypto operations and non-US footprint.
scale_indicators: Investor relations reported 27.7 million funded customers as of May 31, 2026.
Investor relations reported $377 billion total platform assets as of May 31, 2026.
Investor relations notes crypto fair value includes Bitstamp starting June 2025.
regulatory_and_licensing: Public company SEC reporting and investor-relations disclosure.
Brokerage, crypto, futures, and event-contract activities should be mapped to entity-level licenses.
Bitstamp regulatory licenses and registrations should be added to the KYC structure chart.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions flag in the collected public KYC sources.
Regulatory perimeter remains material because products include crypto, brokerage, futures, and event contracts.
banking_relevance: Confirm expected fiat settlement flows after Bitstamp integration.
Update entity chart, licensing footprint, and crypto custody/trading narrative.
Review whether event contracts or private-market access change suitability/compliance expectations.
open_kyc_questions: Which legal entities now control Bitstamp operations and client assets?
Which jurisdictions are active operating markets versus marketing or product-availability regions?
Do expected transaction volumes need to include Bitstamp platform assets and institutional crypto flows?

risk_rationale: Private prediction-market/event-contract business using crypto settlement.
CFTC order identified unregistered off-exchange event-based binary options conduct.
Strategic investment from ICE improves institutional credibility but creates new ownership/control and tokenization questions.

identity.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States

business_model: Prediction market and event-contract platform.
Event markets have included binary yes/no contracts resolved by future events.
Crypto/blockchain smart-contract infrastructure is central to the historic operating model.
ICE investment and tokenization partnership may change institutional distribution and data-commercialization profile.
scale_indicators: CFTC order said Polymarket had offered more than 900 event markets since inception.
ICE official release disclosed a strategic investment of up to $2 billion at an approximately $8 billion pre-investment valuation.
regulatory_and_licensing: CFTC order required a $1.4 million civil monetary penalty and wind-down of non-compliant markets.
Event-contract and gambling-law perimeter should be reviewed by jurisdiction.
QCEX/QCX acquisition and ICE partnership require updated legal-entity and licensing review.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions designation in collected public KYC sources.
Regulatory scrutiny and jurisdiction blocks are material for onboarding and ongoing monitoring.
banking_relevance: Enhanced due diligence should validate business model, legal status, and permitted jurisdictions.
Clarify settlement assets, fiat rails, stablecoin flows, market-makers, and liquidity providers.
Ask for updated investor/control-person schedule after ICE investment.
open_kyc_questions: Which entity is the contracting customer and which entity operates regulated event contracts?
Are users, counterparties, or liquidity providers located in restricted or blocked jurisdictions?
Does ICE receive board, veto, data, tokenization, or control rights?

source_notes.source_name: CFTC order against Blockratize d/b/a Polymarket

risk_rationale: Public retail company with SEC and investor-relations disclosure.
Baseline retail profile is materially changed by bitcoin treasury exposure.
Low inherent AML risk retail profile becomes medium due to digital-asset treasury and financing activity.

identity.headquarters: 625 Westport Parkway, Grapevine, Texas, United States

business_model: Retail gaming and trade-in destination for games, systems, cards, consoles, and accessories.
Ecommerce and retail store operations.
Digital-asset treasury exposure through bitcoin purchase.
scale_indicators: Investor-relations page provides SEC filings and annual/quarterly reports.
Public reporting and market listing make ongoing KYC monitoring scalable.
regulatory_and_licensing: Public-company SEC reporting.
Digital-asset treasury policy should be documented even though GameStop is not a crypto financial intermediary.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions flag in collected public KYC sources.
Bitcoin treasury volatility and custody model require risk review.
banking_relevance: Confirm custody provider, execution desk, wallet controls, insurance, and accounting policy for bitcoin holdings.
Reassess treasury, lending, collateral, and FX opportunity.
Monitor any debt/equity financing used to fund digital-asset purchases.
open_kyc_questions: Who custodies the bitcoin and who can authorize transfers?
Are bitcoin purchases one-off treasury allocation or an ongoing investment policy?
Will financing proceeds be used for additional digital assets?

source_notes.source_name: Investopedia coverage of GameStop bitcoin purchase

risk_rationale: Private crypto exchange and institutional digital-asset platform.
Expanded beyond spot crypto into futures, derivatives, tokenized equities, and fintech/bank embedded trading.
M&A activity adds ownership/control and regulated futures footprint questions.
business_model: Cryptocurrency exchange and institutional digital-asset services.
Custody, trading, staking, wallet, derivatives, tokenized-equity, and embedded crypto-trading offerings.
NinjaTrader acquisition adds retail futures platform and FCM-related footprint.
scale_indicators: Public sources describe Kraken as a major global crypto exchange.
NinjaTrader acquisition was announced at $1.5 billion.
xStocks product creates stock-linked tokenized instruments for non-US customers.
regulatory_and_licensing: Crypto exchange regulatory perimeter should be mapped by operating entity and jurisdiction.
NinjaTrader and derivatives footprint require CFTC/NFA entity review.
xStocks issuer/distributor structure should be reviewed separately from Kraken operating entities.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions designation in collected public KYC sources.
Prior regulatory scrutiny and tokenized-securities perimeter make EDD appropriate.
banking_relevance: Map all regulated entities, issuers, distributors, custody entities, and derivatives entities.
Confirm whether xStocks holders have no underlying equity ownership and identify issuer/custodian obligations.
Reassess expected fiat settlement flows after M&A and product expansion.
open_kyc_questions: Which legal entity is the bank customer versus product distributor?
Which regulator supervises each activity: exchange, custody, staking, derivatives, tokenized securities?
Do any tokenized-equity products create securities, suitability, or custody obligations for AMINA?

risk_rationale: Public financial technology company issuing stablecoins through regulated affiliates.
Stablecoin issuance, reserves, public blockchains, and global settlement rails create financial-crime, sanctions, and reserve-transparency review needs.
Public-company disclosure improves monitoring coverage.
business_model: Global financial technology firm using digital currencies and public blockchains for payments, commerce, and financial applications.
Issuer of USDC and EURC stablecoins through regulated affiliates.
Enterprise and developer services for integrating stablecoins and blockchains into business operations.
scale_indicators: Investor-relations page provides SEC filings, quarterly results, reserve composition, and monthly attestations.
Circle describes itself as building a large stablecoin network.
regulatory_and_licensing: Stablecoins are issued through regulated affiliates.
Reserve composition and monthly attestations are important recurring KYC controls.
MiCA, US stablecoin regulation, Singapore, and other licensing regimes should be mapped by entity.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions designation in collected public KYC sources.
Stablecoin flows require sanctions-screening and blockchain-monitoring controls.
banking_relevance: Payments, FX, treasury, custody, stablecoin settlement, and tokenized cash opportunities.
Monitor reserve composition, redemption mechanics, and regulatory changes.
Confirm entity issuing each product and bank-account flow expectations.
open_kyc_questions: Which affiliate issues each stablecoin and which entity would bank with AMINA?
What are expected reserve, mint, redeem, and treasury flows by jurisdiction?
Which blockchain-monitoring and sanctions controls govern enterprise payments?

risk_rationale: OFAC sanctioned Garantex in April 2022.
Treasury described Russia-linked operations and illicit actor/darknet-market transaction exposure.
Estonian license loss for AML/CFT deficiencies creates a direct regulatory failure signal.

identity.headquarters: Originally Estonia; Treasury described Moscow and St. Petersburg operations

business_model: Virtual currency exchange allowing customers to buy and sell virtual currencies using fiat currencies.
Treasury linked majority operations to Moscow and St. Petersburg.
License and sanctions events supersede ordinary commercial KYC.
scale_indicators: Treasury said over $100 million in transactions were associated with illicit actors and darknet markets.
Treasury identified nearly $6 million from Conti and approximately $2.6 million from Hydra.
regulatory_and_licensing: Estonian FIU license loss in February 2022 after critical AML/CFT deficiencies.
OFAC designation under Russia-related sanctions.
Blocked-property and 50-percent rule implications must be checked before any exposure.
sanctions_adverse_media: Ransomware, darknet-market, Russia sanctions-evasion, and AML/CFT deficiency exposure.
banking_relevance: Do not onboard without sanctions/legal approval.
Screen entity, aliases, related wallets, officers, beneficial owners, and counterparties.
Freeze, reject, or exit any exposure according to sanctions counsel and bank policy.
open_kyc_questions: Is there any direct or indirect exposure to Garantex or related blocked property?
Are counterparties, wallets, or intermediaries connected to Garantex clusters?
Has any historical relationship been exited and documented?

source_notes.source_name: U.S. Treasury OFAC action on Garantex

risk_rationale: Large public tobacco company with inherently sensitive product sector.
OFAC settlement involved North Korea and WMD sanctions exposure through subsidiary and intermediaries.
Historical conduct required major settlement and compliance commitments.
business_model: Tobacco and nicotine products multinational.
Product set includes cigarettes, heated tobacco, vapor, and oral nicotine products.
Large multinational trade, subsidiary, distributor, and sanctions-control perimeter.
scale_indicators: Treasury described BAT as one of the world's largest tobacco companies.
OFAC settlement was the largest ever against a non-financial institution at announcement date.
regulatory_and_licensing: Public-company disclosure in the UK and US ADS context.
Tobacco sector controls, sanctions controls, anti-bribery and anti-corruption controls should be documented.
Subsidiary and distributor screening is central to KYC.
sanctions_adverse_media: OFAC settlement for apparent DPRK and WMD sanctions violations.
Treasury described over $250 million in profits from a North Korean joint venture moving through US financial institutions.
BAT agreed to compliance commitments and investments.
banking_relevance: Enhanced due diligence for sanctioned-country, intermediary, and subsidiary exposure.
Review trade finance restrictions, distributor payments, and high-risk jurisdictions.
Screen subsidiaries, intermediaries, and beneficial owners against sanctions and PEP lists.
open_kyc_questions: Which subsidiaries, distributors, and intermediaries touch high-risk jurisdictions?
What controls prevent indirect sanctioned-country payments through third parties?
Are any historical North Korea-related accounts, counterparties, or claims still open?

source_notes.source_name: U.S. Treasury BAT North Korea settlement

risk_rationale: Public crypto platform with broad institutional, consumer, developer, custody, staking, and derivatives footprint.
Licenses and disclosures show money-transmission and virtual-currency regulatory perimeter across many US jurisdictions.
Country-code domain signal adds market-entry review need.
business_model: Crypto platform serving consumers, institutions, and developers.
Coinbase Prime, custody, exchange, staking, stablecoin infrastructure, developer platform, and global settlement rails.
Public messaging describes an expansion toward an everything exchange covering crypto, equities, derivatives, and prediction markets.
scale_indicators: Investor relations reported $202 billion quarterly trading volume as of March 31, 2026.
Investor relations reported $294 billion assets on platform as of March 31, 2026.
Investor relations reported 4,900+ employees as of March 31, 2026.
regulatory_and_licensing: Coinbase, Inc. license page lists US money-transmitter licenses and virtual-currency licenses/disclosures.
New York listing includes money transmitter and virtual currency business activity license references.
Product set requires entity-by-entity mapping for custody, exchange, derivatives, staking, and developer services.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions designation in collected public KYC sources.
Crypto platform exposure requires sanctions, blockchain analytics, travel rule, fraud, and market-integrity controls.
banking_relevance: Strong institutional custody, trading, treasury, staking, and stablecoin opportunity.
Country-domain signal should be validated as defensive registration or Australia market activity.
Expected transaction flows may include institutional settlements and custody operations.
open_kyc_questions: Which licensed entity would bank with AMINA and for which product line?
Is coinbase.au defensive, pre-launch, or tied to active Australian operations?
What controls govern customer asset custody, staking, sanctions, and institutional settlement flows?

risk_rationale: Large public technology holding company with strong disclosure coverage.
Core AML risk is low-to-medium, but antitrust, privacy, AI infrastructure, cloud, data, cyber, and geopolitical exposure matter for KYC narrative.
Founder voting-control structure should be understood even where public float is broad.
business_model: Public technology holding company with search, advertising, cloud, consumer software, devices, AI, and other bets.
Google Cloud and AI infrastructure activity are relevant for treasury, FX, data-center, and supply-chain flows.
Alphabet investor relations provides SEC filings, governance, proxy, and financial reporting.
scale_indicators: Alphabet investor relations provides SEC filings, quarterly and annual reports, proxy filings, and governance materials.
AI infrastructure signals may change treasury volumes and counterparty regions.
regulatory_and_licensing: Public-company SEC reporting and proxy governance.
Antitrust, privacy, AI, cloud, export-control, tax, and data-sovereignty issues are relevant non-AML KYC context.
Cloud/data-center supply chain may create new high-value cross-border flows.
sanctions_adverse_media: No sanctions designation in collected public KYC sources.
Regulatory scrutiny risk is primarily antitrust, privacy, AI, market-access, and data governance.
banking_relevance: Treasury, FX, cash management, data-center capex, and enterprise technology relationship opportunities.
Monitor AI infrastructure expansion for counterparty, region, and volume changes against baseline.
Review founder voting-control and institutional shareholder context for governance risk.
open_kyc_questions: Does AI infrastructure activity materially change expected treasury volumes or counterparties?
Are new AI hardware, cloud, or data-center counterparties in jurisdictions outside the baseline?
Should KYC activity narrative include AI chip commercialization and TPU infrastructure?
